intro:
The trial of Jesus is the sequence of linked judicial proceedings — first Jewish and religious, then Roman and civil — by which, in the hours between his arrest in Gethsemane and his crucifixion, the Lord was examined, condemned, and handed over to death. It unfolded in two great phases: an ecclesiastical trial before the high priests and the Sanhedrin, and a Roman trial before the governor Pontius Pilate, with an interlude before Herod Antipas. At every stage the proceedings were marked by haste, irregularity, and injustice, yet through them the eternal purpose of God was being accomplished.

In the Jewish phase Jesus was led first to Annas and then to Caiaphas, where the council “were seeking false testimony against Jesus that they might put him to death” (Matthew 26:59–60). When the witnesses failed to agree, the high priest put him under oath, and Jesus answered plainly that he was the Christ, the Son of God, who would be seen “seated at the right hand of Power and coming on the clouds of heaven” (Mark 14:61–62). On this confession he was charged with blasphemy and judged “deserving death” (Matthew 26:65–66), then mocked and struck, while in the courtyard Peter denied him. At daybreak the council reconvened to ratify the verdict (Luke 22:66–71).

Because the Sanhedrin could not lawfully execute, they brought him to Pilate, recasting the religious charge as political sedition: that he claimed to be “Christ, a king” and forbade tribute to Caesar (Luke 23:2). To Pilate’s question Jesus confessed, “My kingdom is not of this world” (John 18:36). Three times the governor declared, “I find no guilt in him” (John 18:38; John 19:6); he sent him to Herod (Luke 23:7) and offered to release him in place of Barabbas. Yet, yielding to the crowd, Pilate scourged him, presented him with “Behold the man!” (John 19:5), washed his hands (Matthew 27:24), and delivered him to be crucified.

The trial is the supreme miscarriage of human justice: false witnesses, a foregone verdict, and a judge who condemns the one he has thrice pronounced innocent. So the Righteous One fulfills the prophet’s word, “By oppression and judgment he was taken away” (Isaiah 53:8), led “like a lamb that is led to the slaughter” (Isaiah 53:7). The release of Barabbas, a guilty man freed while the innocent is condemned, paints in miniature the heart of the gospel: the sinless taking the sinner’s place. And what man meant as injustice God had ordained for salvation — Christ was delivered up “according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God” (Acts 2:23; cf. Acts 4:27–28).

In his trial the Judge of all the earth submits to be judged, and the King reigns from the place of apparent defeat. He is condemned for confessing the truth of who he is, so that those who deserve condemnation might be acquitted. The verdict of Caiaphas and Pilate was overturned by God himself in the resurrection, which vindicated the Crucified and declared him Son of God in power. For the believer the trial of Jesus is both the ground of justification and a deep comfort to all who suffer unjustly: the Lord himself stood falsely accused and was not ashamed.
